description: Per-edit-type priors. Each type carries target pacing, sync preference, transition bias, and emotion palette. Values distilled from public editor guidance (animemusicvideos.org, studiobinder.com, editor forums) plus the 148-video reference corpus. The planner blends these with corpus priors — type priors set the TARGET, corpus priors calibrate against real examples.

action.label: Action / Hype
action.description: Fast, beat-locked, high-energy edits. Think multifandom action compilations and combat sequences synced to drops.
action.craft_notes: Hard cuts dominate; variety comes from beat-aligned flash and whip transitions on drops. Use match cuts across fandoms to keep motion continuity. Movement intensity matters more than narrative coherence within a sequence. Heavy reliance on drop-timed impacts and sub-boom SFX.

emotional.label: Emotional / Tender
emotional.description: Slow, breathing edits that let moments land. Used for grief, loss, quiet triumph, reflective character moments.
emotional.craft_notes: Let shots breathe 5+ seconds. Crossfades carry the emotional register. Internal sync (a tear landing on a musical swell) beats beat-sync. Eyeline and gaze shots matter more than action. Low saturation, cool color palette. Dialogue cues often carry more weight than the song's drops.

tribute.label: Character Tribute
tribute.description: Character-focused edit celebrating a single character's arc across their appearances. Structured around the character's emotional journey.
tribute.craft_notes: Three-act structure: introduce character → escalate their struggle → resolution/triumph or fall. Keep ONE character on screen for most shots. Act transitions often use dissolves; within-act uses hard cuts. Reaction shots carry emotional beats.

shipping.label: Shipping / Romance
shipping.description: Romantic pairing edit. Soft, tender, dissolve-heavy. Celebrates a relationship arc — meet-cute through connection to intimacy or loss.
shipping.craft_notes: Pairing must share screen time — two-shot compositions and eyeline matches dominate. Dissolves signal emotional continuity. Lyric sync is strong here: visuals should support the lyric's meaning. Warm color palette, softer grade than action. Reaction shots of one partner to the other are the core language.

speed_amv.label: Speed AMV / Anime Speed-Edit
speed_amv.description: Machine-gun pacing, heavy effects, anime-first aesthetic. Impact frames, zoom-ins, flash transitions.
speed_amv.craft_notes: Speed ramps, impact frames, zoom punches on every downbeat. Overlay effects, light leaks, chromatic aberration are expected. Lyric sync rarely; pure beat/cut sync drives everything. Anime hand-drawn style benefits from higher saturation. Length typically 30-90s — shorter than most fandom types.

cinematic.label: Cinematic / Slow-Burn
cinematic.description: Baseline-paced film-style edit. Shots breathe. Motivated cuts. Emphasis on storytelling over synchronization.
cinematic.craft_notes: 4-6s average shot length matches modern feature film baseline. Cuts motivated by performance or narrative beats, NOT music. Internal sync (action-on-music-peak) beats cut-sync. Color grade is feature-film-adjacent (teal shadows, orange skin tones). Dialogue/VO often layered in.

comedy.label: Comedy / Meme
comedy.description: Timing-driven humor edit. Comic beats, punchline cuts, intentional rule-breaking. Structure exists to serve the joke.
comedy.craft_notes: Pacing serves the punchline — setup shots can breathe long, punch cuts hit hard and short. Freeze frames, record-scratches, and smash cuts are core vocabulary. Rules get broken intentionally. Audio gags (dialogue rips, meme sounds) often carry more weight than music. Brighter grade than action/emotional.

hype_trailer.label: Hype / Trailer
hype_trailer.description: Rising-tension edit that builds across three acts — quiet intro → escalation → explosive payoff. Structured like a movie trailer.
hype_trailer.craft_notes: Shot duration decreases across the three acts: act 1 averages 3s (establishing), act 2 averages 2s (escalation), act 3 averages 1s (climax). Sub-boom impacts mark each act transition. Title cards between acts. Lip-sync dialogue lands on the quiet intro to set stakes.
